milestone_description: Elevate RASS from a functional RAG proof-of-concept to a retrieval system with measurable, state-of-the-art quality. Introduce a modular retrieval pipeline, cross-encoder reranking, HyDE query expansion, structured citations, and an automated evaluation harness with tracked metrics.

issues.title: [Phase C] Build a modular, pluggable multi-stage retrieval pipeline
issues.body:
## Problem

The current retrieval logic in `rass-engine-service/index.js` is a monolithic function. All stages — query embedding, OpenSearch KNN search, keyword search, result merging, parent fetch — are tightly coupled in a single code path. This makes it impossible to:
- Swap in a different retrieval strategy without rewriting core logic
- Add pre-retrieval (query rewriting) or post-retrieval (reranking) steps cleanly
- A/B test different retrieval configurations
- Measure latency per stage

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Define the pipeline abstraction
Create `rass-engine-service/src/retrieval/` with:
- `Pipeline.js` — executes an ordered array of `Stage` objects, threading a `context` object through each
- `Stage.js` — base class: `async run(context) → context`
- `context.js` — shape: `{ query, userId, kbId, queryEmbedding, candidateChunks, rankedChunks, parentDocs, config }`

### 2. Implement concrete stage classes
- `EmbedQueryStage.js` — embeds the raw query string using the configured provider
- `HybridSearchStage.js` — executes KNN + keyword OpenSearch query, returns `candidateChunks`
- `ParentFetchStage.js` — fetches parent documents from Redis for each candidate chunk's `parentId`
- `DeduplicateStage.js` — removes duplicate parent documents (same content from multiple child hits)
- `RerankStage.js` — applies cross-encoder reranking (see separate issue); no-op if reranker not configured
- `TopKSelectStage.js` — selects top-K parents for context window

### 3. Compose the pipeline in a factory
```js
// rass-engine-service/src/retrieval/createPipeline.js
export function createPipeline(config) {
  return new Pipeline([
    new EmbedQueryStage(config),
    new HybridSearchStage(config),
    new ParentFetchStage(config),
    new DeduplicateStage(),
    new RerankStage(config),
    new TopKSelectStage(config),
  ]);
}
```

### 4. Instrument each stage
- Each stage records its wall-clock time in `context.stageTimes`
- Log stage times as structured JSON at INFO level

### 5. Migrate the existing `/stream-ask` handler to use the pipeline

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The retrieval flow is expressed as an ordered list of `Stage` instances in `createPipeline.js`
- [ ] Each stage can be individually unit-tested by providing a mock `context`
- [ ] Stage execution times are logged as structured JSON
- [ ] Retrieval results are identical to the existing behavior (verified by running the TruLens eval suite)
- [ ] A stage can be disabled/replaced via config without modifying any stage implementations
- [ ] End-to-end latency does not regress by more than 10 % compared to the current implementation

issues.title: [Phase C] Integrate cross-encoder reranking for improved retrieval precision
issues.body:
## Problem

Hybrid search (KNN + BM25) retrieves a set of candidate documents ranked by embedding similarity and keyword overlap. These ranking signals are noisy — a semantically similar but topically irrelevant chunk can score high. Cross-encoder reranking uses a separate model that jointly encodes the query and each candidate to produce a more accurate relevance score, consistently improving NDCG and precision@k in published benchmarks.

Without reranking, RASS sends lower-quality context to the LLM, increasing hallucination risk and reducing answer faithfulness.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Implement `RerankStage` with provider abstraction
Create `rass-engine-service/src/retrieval/reranking/`:
- `RerankProvider.js` — interface: `async rerank(query, documents) → scored documents`
- `CohereRerankProvider.js` — calls `cohere.rerank()` API
- `LocalCrossEncoderProvider.js` — calls an optional local Python microservice running `cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2` via ONNX
- `NoopRerankProvider.js` — returns documents unchanged (default)

### 2. Add configuration
```yaml
# config.yml
RERANK_PROVIDER: cohere          # cohere | local | none
RERANK_TOP_N: 5
COHERE_API_KEY: ${COHERE_API_KEY}
```

### 3. Wire into the retrieval pipeline
- `RerankStage` reads `config.RERANK_PROVIDER`, instantiates the correct provider
- Input: `context.candidateChunks` (e.g., top 20 from hybrid search)
- Output: `context.rankedChunks` sorted by rerank score (top N)
- Log rerank scores at DEBUG level for observability

### 4. Evaluate reranking impact
- Run TruLens evaluation suite with and without reranking
- Record `context_relevance`, `answer_faithfulness`, `answer_relevance` for both configs
- Document delta in `evaluation/results/reranking_impact.md`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `RerankStage` is wired into the pipeline and activated when `RERANK_PROVIDER != none`
- [ ] Cohere Rerank provider is implemented and tested with a live API key in CI (optional)
- [ ] `NoopRerankProvider` is the default; retrieval quality is unchanged when provider is `none`
- [ ] Rerank scores are visible in structured logs at DEBUG level
- [ ] TruLens evaluation shows measurable improvement in `context_relevance` when reranking is enabled (target: ≥ 5 % relative improvement on the test set)
- [ ] Unit tests for each provider using mocked API responses

issues.title: [Phase C] Implement HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) query expansion
issues.body:
## Problem

Short, keyword-sparse user queries often retrieve poor candidates because their embeddings are far from the dense document embeddings in the vector space. HyDE addresses this by using the LLM to generate a *hypothetical* answer document for the query, then embedding *that* document for retrieval. This dramatically improves recall for factual and definitional queries.

A `hydeGenerator.js` file already exists in `rass-engine-service/` but appears to be unused in the main retrieval path.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Audit and refactor `hydeGenerator.js`
- Review the existing implementation for correctness
- Ensure it accepts `(query, llmConfig)` and returns a hypothetical document string
- Add timeout and error handling (fall back to original query on failure)

### 2. Create `HydeQueryExpansionStage`
- Insert before `EmbedQueryStage` in the pipeline
- If `HYDE_ENABLED: true` in config, call the HyDE generator and replace `context.query` with the concatenation of original query + hypothetical document for embedding purposes
- Store original query separately in context for citation/display purposes

### 3. Configuration
```yaml
HYDE_ENABLED: false        # opt-in
HYDE_MAX_TOKENS: 200       # keep hypothetical doc short
```

### 4. Evaluation
- Measure recall@k (percentage of ground-truth chunks retrieved) with and without HyDE using the TruLens test set
- Document findings in `evaluation/results/hyde_impact.md`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `HydeQueryExpansionStage` is implemented and inserted at the correct pipeline position
- [ ] When `HYDE_ENABLED: false`, the pipeline is identical to the current behavior
- [ ] HyDE generator failures gracefully fall back to the original query (no crash)
- [ ] Latency overhead of HyDE (one extra LLM call) is logged and surfaced in stage timing
- [ ] TruLens recall@5 with `HYDE_ENABLED: true` meets or exceeds baseline (documented in eval results)
- [ ] Unit test: mock LLM returns a hypothetical doc → verify embedding input is enriched

issues.title: [Phase C] Implement structured citations with source attribution and confidence
issues.body:
## Problem

RASS currently streams answer text with inline citation markers (e.g., `[1]`) but the citation resolution is fragile: citations are extracted post-hoc from the LLM's text output, and the mapping between citation markers and source documents is not always reliable. There is no:
- Confidence score associated with each citation
- Structured citation object returned to the frontend
- Deep-link to the specific page or section within the source document
- Verification that the cited text actually appears in the retrieved context

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Design a structured `Citation` type
```ts
interface Citation {
  index: number;           // [1], [2], etc.
  documentId: string;      // FK to Document registry
  documentName: string;
  chunkId: string;         // OpenSearch document ID
  relevanceScore: number;  // from hybrid search or reranker
  excerpt: string;         // 200-char snippet from the chunk
  pageNumber?: number;     // if parseable from metadata
  uploadedAt: string;
}
```

### 2. Emit structured citations in the SSE stream
- After the generation is complete (or in a separate SSE event type), emit a `citations` event: `data: {"type":"citations","citations":[...]}`
- Frontend parses this event and renders a structured citations panel

### 3. Verify citation grounding
- After LLM generation, extract quoted or referenced sentences from the answer
- For each citation, check whether the corresponding chunk text contains (or is highly similar to) the cited statement
- Set a `grounded: boolean` field on each citation

### 4. Frontend citation panel
- Render citations as expandable cards below the answer
- Each card shows: document name, excerpt, relevance score, and a link to view the full document
- Ungrounded citations are flagged with a warning icon

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each answer stream emits a structured `citations` SSE event with typed citation objects
- [ ] Every citation includes `documentId`, `documentName`, `excerpt`, and `relevanceScore`
- [ ] `pageNumber` is populated when the document loader provides page metadata (PDF)
- [ ] Frontend renders a citation panel that matches the structured event
- [ ] Grounding verification runs after generation; ungrounded citations display a warning
- [ ] Regression test: submit a known query and assert that the citation structure is correct and complete

issues.title: [Phase C] Build an automated RAG evaluation harness with tracked metrics
issues.body:
## Problem

The existing `evaluation/trulens_evaluator/evaluate.py` script runs TruLens evaluations but:
- Must be run manually — there is no CI integration
- Results are not persisted or compared across runs
- There is no regression detection (i.e., no way to know if a code change degraded retrieval quality)
- The test set is ad-hoc and undocumented

Without a systematic evaluation harness, it is impossible to confidently claim that retrieval improvements are real or that regressions are caught.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Define a curated test set
Create `evaluation/datasets/test_set.json`:
```json
[
  {
    "id": "q001",
    "query": "What is the recommended dosage for metformin?",
    "expected_answer_contains": ["500mg", "twice daily"],
    "relevant_document_ids": ["doc_abc123"]
  },
  ...
]
```
Include at least 20 diverse queries covering factual recall, multi-hop reasoning, and out-of-scope questions.

### 2. Refactor the evaluation runner
- `evaluation/run_eval.py` — CLI entrypoint: loads test set, runs RASS `/stream-ask` for each query, measures TruLens metrics
- Metrics: `context_relevance`, `answer_faithfulness`, `answer_relevance`, `recall@5`, `latency_p50`, `latency_p95`
- Output: `evaluation/results/run_<timestamp>.json` with all per-query scores + aggregate statistics

### 3. Add regression detection
- `evaluation/compare_runs.py` — compares the latest run against the previous baseline
- Fails with exit code 1 if any metric degrades by more than a configurable threshold (default: 5 %)

### 4. CI integration
- Add a `weekly-eval` GitHub Actions workflow that:
  - Spins up the RASS stack with `docker compose up -d`
  - Ingests the evaluation corpus
  - Runs `evaluation/run_eval.py`
  - Runs `evaluation/compare_runs.py`
  - Publishes results as a GitHub Actions summary

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `evaluation/datasets/test_set.json` contains ≥ 20 labeled queries
- [ ] `evaluation/run_eval.py` runs end-to-end without manual intervention and produces a JSON results file
- [ ] Results include all six metrics for each query and aggregate mean/p50/p95
- [ ] `evaluation/compare_runs.py` correctly identifies regressions > 5 % and exits non-zero
- [ ] A GitHub Actions workflow runs the eval suite weekly and publishes a summary
- [ ] Results from at least two reference runs are checked in to `evaluation/results/` as baselines
